note_data.title: Enzymes
keyPoints: **Enzymes** are biological catalysts that speed up chemical reactions without being used up
Enzymes are **proteins** with a specific three-dimensional shape determined by their amino acid sequence
The **active site** has a complementary shape to the substrate and forms an enzyme-substrate complex
Enzymes work by **lowering activation energy** required for a reaction to occur
**Temperature** affects enzyme activity - increasing temperature increases kinetic energy and collision frequency until optimum temperature
Above optimum temperature, enzymes become **denatured** - the active site changes shape permanently and cannot bind to substrate
**pH** affects enzyme activity - each enzyme has an optimum pH, extreme pH denatures enzymes
**Substrate concentration** affects rate of reaction until all active sites are occupied (saturation point)
Enzymes show **specificity** - each enzyme catalyses only one type of reaction due to the specific shape of its active site

definitions.definition: A biological catalyst that speeds up chemical reactions without being used up, made of protein
The part of an enzyme molecule with a complementary shape to the substrate where the substrate binds
The substance that an enzyme acts upon during a chemical reaction
When an enzyme's active site permanently changes shape due to high temperature or extreme pH, making it non-functional
A substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being used up or changed in the reaction

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give detailed account of what happens step by step
Give similarities AND differences between two things

modelAnswer.question: Explain why enzyme activity decreases at high temperatures. (3 marks)
modelAnswer.answer: At high temperatures, enzymes become denatured. The high temperature breaks the bonds holding the enzyme's shape together, causing the active site to change shape permanently. The substrate can no longer bind to the active site as they are no longer complementary in shape.
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Substrate cannot bind to active site/no longer complementary

commonMistakes: Never say 'enzymes die' — always say 'enzymes become denatured'
Students often confuse optimum temperature with body temperature - optimum varies for different enzymes
Never say 'enzymes are used up' — enzymes are reusable and remain unchanged after reaction
Students forget that denaturation is permanent - don't say enzymes can be 'fixed' after denaturation
Don't say 'active site breaks' — say 'active site changes shape'
examinerTips: This topic appears almost every year in Paper 2, especially enzyme-temperature graphs
Always use the term 'complementary shape' when describing enzyme-substrate binding
Questions on digestive enzymes (amylase, pepsin, lipase) are very common
Draw enzyme-substrate complex diagrams clearly showing shape change
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Lock and key model showing enzyme with complementary-shaped active site, substrate binding to form enzyme-substrate complex, then products released with enzyme unchanged
